title: Chapter 2: The Prescription
content: <!-- Speed: Pivot | D3+D12 -->

The fluorescent tube above the waiting-room chair had a flicker in it. Not a full strobe — just enough to live at the edge of perception, like a mosquito in a dark room. Wren pulled her headphones off one ear, then put them back. The magazines on the table were from 2023. A plastic fern sat in the corner, dust layered on its leaves like frost.
She'd been here eleven minutes. She'd counted.

The receptionist hadn't looked at her when she signed in. That was fine. That was preferable. The less looking, the better. The chair was hard plastic, cold through her hoodie, and the air smelled like artificial lavender and something sharper underneath — disinfectant, maybe, or the ghost of a thousand anxious afternoons.
"Ms. Walker?" The receptionist's voice was flat. "Dr. Vance will see you now."
Down the hallway. More fluorescence. A framed print on the wall — one of those generic pastoral scenes, rolling hills, no people. Of course. No people. Wren's boots squeaked on the linoleum.
She stopped.

The door was open. Dr. Lena Vance sat behind a desk that held nothing — no family photos, no coffee-stained mugs, not even a pen out of place. A laptop, closed. A legal pad, blank. A nameplate that read DR. L. VANCE in small sans-serif letters.

Vance herself was early-forties, silver streaking through dark hair pulled into a twist so tight it looked architectural. She wore a white coat over a charcoal blouse, and clinical gloves that never came off — latex, nitrile, something. Pale against the desk. Clean.
"Please, sit." Vance's voice was room temperature. Not warm. Not cold. Measured.
Wren sat. The chair facing the desk was softer than the waiting-room one, which somehow made it worse. She sank. She didn't want to sink.

"Your referral indicates generalized anxiety disorder with comorbid social phobia." Vance consulted the laptop screen, having opened it without Wren noticing. "Prescribing history shows two SSRIs discontinued due to side effects. One benzodiazepine — discontinued. One beta-blocker — also discontinued."
Wren tugged her sleeve over her wrist. "The SSRIs made me foggy. I need to draw. I can't — foggy doesn't work for —" She stopped. "Never mind."
"I understand you're an illustrator."
It wasn't a question. Wren nodded anyway.

"Professional creative work does require clarity." Vance folded her gloved hands. The latex made a soft, dry sound. "I'm going to be direct with you, Ms. Walker, because I respect your time and your intelligence. The pharmaceutical landscape for anxiety has been stagnant for the better part of a decade. SSRIs, SNRIs, benzos — they're blunt instruments. They treat the smoke, not the fire."
Wren stared at the blank legal pad. She could feel Vance's attention on her like diffuse lamplight.

"I'm involved in a clinical trial for a compound called ANX-00. It operates on a different pathway entirely — not serotonin reuptake, not GABA modulation. It targets the neurovascular coupling between the prefrontal cortex and the amygdala. In simple terms, it doesn't suppress the anxiety signal. It adjusts how visible that signal is to the rest of your neural architecture."
The words hung in the sterile air. Wren's pulse ticked. Visible. She noticed the word and then didn't know why she'd noticed it.

"Off-label," Vance continued, "but the results are remarkable. My subjects report significant reduction in social reactivity within forty-eight hours. No cognitive fog. No weight gain. No emotional flattening."
"How many subjects?"
"Adequate for clinical significance."

Which meant not many. Wren wasn't stupid. She'd read enough about trials to know the language of hedge — "adequate," "significant," "emerging data." But Vance's voice didn't hedge. It delivered each phrase like a scalpel stroke, clean and sure.
"I just — what are the side effects?"
"Minimal. Some subjects report mild perceptual adjustment in the first seventy-two hours — slight visual disturbances, spatial recalibration. Transient. Benign."
Perceptual adjustment. Another beautiful phrase for something Wren couldn't picture. She pulled at her sleeve again. The fabric was pilling at the cuff.
"And if I say no?"

"Then we explore conventional options." Vance's expression did not change. Not even a flicker. "I'm offering you a choice, not a mandate. ANX-00 is not FDA-approved. It is experimental. I'm required to tell you that, and I'm choosing to be transparent about the fact that I believe it will help you. I don't extend this offer to every patient."

There it was — the lure. Not everyone gets this. You're special. Wren heard it clearly, and she resented it, and she was already reaching for it anyway because the alternative was another round of pills that made her feel like she was watching her own life through frosted glass.
Vance opened a desk drawer. Inside: an amber prescription bottle, small enough to fit in a closed fist. She set it on the blotter between them.
The pill inside was white. Small. Featureless. A tiny compressed moon.

Wren looked at it. Her heart was a fist knocking against her sternum. The room was too bright, too clean, too free of anything human, and the pill sat there like a period at the end of a sentence she hadn't finished writing.
"I'll think about it," she said.

"Of course." Vance slid a consent form — two pages, dense type — next to the bottle. "Take the form with you. The starter dose is inside — one tablet, twenty milligrams. If you choose to proceed, take it with food. Avoid alcohol for the first week. Report any perceptual changes to my office immediately."
Immediately. That word again.
Wren picked up the bottle. It was lighter than she expected. She put it in her hoodie pocket, felt it rest against her hip like a small cold stone.

"One more thing." Vance's gloved fingers tapped the desk once — just once. "ANX-00 is most effective when the subject does not discontinue mid-regimen. Once you begin, I strongly recommend finishing the full course. The neurological recalibration is most stable when completed as designed."
"As designed by who?"
"By me." No hesitation. No modesty. Just fact.

Wren left. The hallway was longer going out. The receptionist didn't look up. The waiting room had cycled through two more patients — a man with a shaved head gripping a magazine he wasn't reading, a woman with mascara tracks on her cheeks.

Outside, the sky had turned. Rain was coming — she could smell it on the asphalt, metallic and clean. Her car sat at the far end of the lot, a dented gray Civic with a parking ticket she hadn't paid for flapping under the wiper.

She walked. The rain started before she reached the car — fat drops that hit the pavement in dark circles the size of quarters. By the time she closed the door, the windshield was streaked and the world outside had gone soft and distant.
She sat.

The bottle was still in her pocket. She took it out and held it up to the gray light. The pill inside rolled against the plastic like a marble in a jar. White. Small. A nothing pill. A pill that could be a tic tac or a breath mint or anything else that didn't carry the weight of a neurovascular whatever and a doctor with clinical gloves that never came off.
Her phone buzzed. A text from her roommate, Kayla: how'd it go???
Wren typed back: Got a new med.
Kayla: !!! what kind
Experimental, she didn't type. Instead: The good kind hopefully.

She set the phone face-down on the passenger seat. Rain drummed the roof. The car smelled like old coffee and the library-book vanilla of a paperback she'd left in the back seat for three months. She cracked the amber bottle open.
The pill sat in her palm. Light as a thought.

Her whole life, anxiety had been the weather — not a storm, just the atmosphere. Always there. Always the pressure of it against her skin. She couldn't remember what it felt like to walk into a room without scanning the exits first. She couldn't remember a morning when her heart didn't race before she opened her eyes.
What's the worst that could happen.

She almost laughed. Full Gen Z irony, standing at the edge of a cliff and grinning. What's the worst that could happen — she could turn invisible? She could melt? She could finally, actually, disappear?
The pill went in.
She swallowed.
It was small and it went down easy and the rain kept falling and the windshield kept blurring and the world outside kept being the same world it had always been, gray and wet and loud and too much.
But something had changed.

She could feel it, or she imagined she could — a loosening, like a knot in her chest that had been pulled so tight for so long she'd stopped noticing it was there. The knot loosened. Just a little. Just enough to notice the absence of tension.
She sat in the car in the parking lot and the rain fell and she had swallowed the pill and there was no going back now.
The windshield wipers squeaked when she turned them on. She pulled out of the lot. The road was dark with rain. Nobody looked at her at the stoplight.
Nobody looked at her at all.
